evidence_rationale: Q47 forms the core doctrinal treatment of prudence itself.
evidence_text: Wherefore it is manifest that prudence belongs directly to the cognitive, and not to the sensitive faculty, because by the latter we know nothing but what is within reach and offers itself to the senses: while to obtain knowledge of the future from knowledge of the present or past, which pertains to prudence , belongs properly to the reason, because this is done by a process of comparison
subject_label: II-II q.47 — Prudence, considered in itself

evidence_rationale: Q48 is the tract's organizing article for integral parts.
evidence_text: Parts are of three kinds, namely, "integral," as wall, roof, and foundations are parts of a house
subject_label: II-II q.48 — The parts of prudence

evidence_rationale: Q48 is the tract's organizing article for potential parts.
evidence_text: and " potential ," as the nutritive and sensitive powers are parts of the soul
subject_label: II-II q.48 — The parts of prudence

evidence_rationale: Q48 is the tract's organizing article for subjective parts.
evidence_text: "subjective," as ox and lion are parts of animal
subject_label: II-II q.48 — The parts of prudence

evidence_rationale: Q49 unfolds the integral parts article by article.
evidence_text: i, 1, and therefore prudence requires the memory of many things
subject_label: II-II q.49 — Each quasi-integral part of prudence

evidence_rationale: Q50 unfolds the subjective parts article by article.
evidence_text: Hence prudence in its special and most perfect sense, belongs to a king who is charged with the government of a city or kingdom: for which reason a species of prudence is reckoned to be regnative.
subject_label: II-II q.50 — The subjective parts of prudence

evidence_rationale: Q51 unfolds the potential parts article by article.
evidence_text: But euboulia (deliberating well) signifies goodness of counsel, for it is derived from the eu , good , and boule , counsel, being "a good counsel" or rather "a disposition to take good counsel." Hence it is evident that euboulia (deliberating well) is a human virtue .
subject_label: II-II q.51 — The virtues which are connected with prudence

evidence_text: As stated above ( I-II:68:1 ), the gifts of the Holy Ghost are dispositions whereby the soul is rendered amenable to the motion of the Holy Ghost . Now God moves everything according to the mode of the thing moved: thus He moves the corporeal creature through
subject_label: II-II q.52 — The gift of counsel

evidence_rationale: Q53 begins the tract on imprudence and allied defects.
evidence_text: Imprudence may be taken in two ways, first, as a privation, secondly, as a contrary

evidence_rationale: Q54 continues the tract on prudential defects through negligence.
evidence_text: Negligence denotes lack of due solicitude

evidence_rationale: Q55 treats counterfeit or misdirected prudence-like forms.
evidence_text: Hence prudence of the flesh signifies properly the prudence of a man who looks upon carnal goods as the last end of his life
subject_label: II-II q.55 — Vices opposed to prudence by way of resemblance

evidence_rationale: Q56 gathers the precept material for prudence and its opposite vices.
evidence_text: Article 5, Reply to Objection 1 ) when we were treating of precepts, the commandments of the decalogue being given to the whole people, are a matter of common knowledge to all, as coming under the purview of natural reason
subject_label: II-II q.56 — The precepts relating to prudence
